They could have been more upfront about.note: hey should have been open about it from the beginning

It can't have been easy for them..note: is a phrase expressing empathy and strong deduction that a person or group went through a very difficult or challenging experience

customSentences.text: Small changes tend to add up over time, even when you can't see them straight away.
I've been trying to get into the habit of going to bed at the same time every night.
We tend to overestimate what we can do in a day and underestimate what we can do in a year.

glossary.meaning: judge as more / less than something really is

customSentences.text: It's not really about setting bigger goals — it's more about building better systems.
Every action you take is a kind of vote for the person you're becoming.
The trick is to make the new habit so easy that you almost can't say no.

glossary.meaning: the key / the clever part is

customSentences.text: Once it becomes part of your routine, you barely have to think about it.
Most people give up just before they would have seen real progress.
Your environment shapes your behaviour more than you'd probably like to admit.
If you want to change what you do, you might need to change who you think you are.
Two minutes a day is often enough to get the ball rolling.
Honestly, the hardest part is usually just getting started.
You don't really rise to the level of your goals — you fall to the level of your systems.

glossary.word: rise / fall to the level of
glossary.meaning: perform up to / down to a standard

customSentences.text: It might be worth stacking a new habit on top of one you already have.

glossary.meaning: add / pile on (here: chain habits together)

customSentences.text: It's worth keeping track of your progress, even if it feels a bit silly at first.
I'd rather make a tiny bit of progress every day than burn out trying to do too much at once.
When you keep showing up, the results tend to take care of themselves.
It's been said that you become the average of the five people you spend the most time with.
I came across this idea a few years ago and it really stuck with me.
There isn't really one big decision that changes your life — it's the cumulative effect that matters.
If you can't seem to stick with a habit, the friction is probably too high.
Just because you fell off doesn't mean you have to start over from scratch.
Missing once might be an accident, but missing twice could be the start of a new habit.
Don't break the chain — and if you do, try not to break it twice in a row.

glossary.meaning: interrupt a streak / give up the run

customSentences.text: It turns out that motivation is overrated; consistency is what actually moves the needle.

glossary.meaning: given more value than it deserves

customSentences.text: Bad habits tend to creep in when you're not paying attention.
The cue, the craving, the response, and the reward — that's the loop you've got to be aware of.
I'd say the key is to make it obvious, attractive, easy, and satisfying.
Once you've identified the trigger, you can start to interrupt the pattern.
I'm a firm believer that environment beats willpower nine times out of ten.
The thing about discipline is that it tends to get easier the more you practise it.
Identity-based habits stick because they're tied to who you are, not just what you want.
I don't think anyone's really got it all figured out — we're all just trying to get a little better.
Going from zero to one is usually harder than going from one to ten.
Sometimes you just have to show up, even when you really don't feel like it.
Letting yourself off the hook one time can quickly become a slippery slope.

glossary.meaning: excuse yourself / not hold yourself accountable
course of action leading to bad outcomes

customSentences.text: I've come to realise that small wins matter more than big breakthroughs.
It might seem trivial, but those tiny improvements really do compound over time.
Once you've built up some momentum, it's easier to keep going than to stop.
If I were you, I'd start with whatever feels almost too easy to skip.
I'm going to give him the benefit of the doubt for now.

glossary.word: give someone the benefit of the doubt
glossary.meaning: choose to believe someone is honest/right when you're not 100% sure

customSentences.text: To be fair, she hasn't really had a chance to explain herself yet.
I wouldn't read too much into it if I were you.

glossary.meaning: find meaning that isn't really there

customSentences.text: That's not what I meant — let me put it differently.
I have a feeling something's off, but I can't quite explain why.
We've been going back and forth on this for weeks now.
I'd hate to put you on the spot, but what's your take?
To cut a long story short, we ended up cancelling the trip.

glossary.word: to cut a long story short
glossary.meaning: to summarise briefly / skip the details

customSentences.text: It looks like we'll have to make do with what we've got.
I've been keeping an eye on the situation, just in case.
Now that you mention it, I do remember something about that.
I don't want to jump the gun, but it's looking promising.

glossary.meaning: act too soon / before the right time

customSentences.text: Let's not get ahead of ourselves — there's still a lot to do.
I'm willing to give it a shot if you are.
To be honest, I'm not sure that's a hill worth dying on.

glossary.meaning: an issue important enough to fight hard for

customSentences.text: I'd rather not get involved in this if I can help it.
Could we possibly push the meeting back a bit?
I'd hate to leave you hanging, so let me know either way.

glossary.meaning: fail to give a needed response / keep someone waiting

customSentences.text: You'd think they would have figured it out by now.

glossary.meaning: one would expect (used with mild frustration)

customSentences.text: I'll admit, I didn't see that one coming.
Looking on the bright side, at least we learned something.
I'm trying to figure out where things went wrong.
The way I see it, we have two reasonable options.
It's a tough call, but I'd lean towards the safer one.
Sometimes you've just got to roll with the punches.

glossary.meaning: adapt to whatever happens / accept difficulties calmly

customSentences.text: I don't want to come off as ungrateful, but I expected more.

glossary.meaning: appear to be / give the impression of

customSentences.text: That's water under the bridge as far as I'm concerned.

glossary.meaning: a past problem that doesn't matter anymore

customSentences.text: I wouldn't lose any sleep over it if I were you.
They've been giving me the runaround for months.

glossary.meaning: deliberately delay or avoid giving a clear answer

customSentences.text: To put it bluntly, we can't afford another mistake.

glossary.meaning: to say it directly / without softening

customSentences.text: I'm in no rush — take your time.
Off the top of my head, I'd say around fifty.

glossary.word: off the top of my head
glossary.meaning: without thinking carefully / as an estimate

customSentences.text: I'd say it's worth giving them a second chance.
It crossed my mind, but I dismissed the idea pretty quickly.
I'm not going to pretend I understand everything yet.
Don't get me wrong — I'm not blaming you.
I might be reading between the lines, but it sounds personal.

glossary.meaning: understand a hidden meaning / what isn't said directly

customSentences.text: It depends on how strict they want to be about it.
I'd take that with a pinch of salt if I were you.

glossary.word: take it with a pinch of salt
glossary.meaning: don't fully believe it / be sceptical

customSentences.text: We should really nip this in the bud before it gets worse.

glossary.meaning: stop it early before it grows into a problem

customSentences.text: There's a thin line between being honest and being rude.

glossary.meaning: a small difference separating two things

customSentences.text: I'll be the first to admit I was wrong about that.
Let's not beat around the bush — what do you actually want?

glossary.meaning: avoid the main point / talk indirectly

customSentences.text: He's been bending over backwards to make things right.

glossary.meaning: make a great effort to help / please someone

customSentences.text: You can't please everyone, no matter how hard you try.
I get where you're coming from, but I still have my doubts.
I'd take a rain check, if that's alright with you.

glossary.meaning: politely postpone an invitation to another time

customSentences.text: Honestly, that sounds like more trouble than it's worth.

glossary.meaning: not worth the effort it requires

customSentences.text: We're not seeing eye to eye on this, are we?
Let's call it a day and pick it up tomorrow.
